data.title: These Analysts Cut Their Forecasts On Wealthfront After Q4 Earnings
data.description:
Wealthfront Corp (NASDAQ:WLTH) reported mixed results for the fourth quarter on Wednesday.

The company posted quarterly losses of $1.31 per share which missed the analyst consensus estimate of losses of $1.17 per share. The company reported quarterly sales of $96.136 million which beat the analyst consensus estimate of $92.351 million.

David Fortunato, CEO, President & Director, said, “We capped off a milestone year in the fourth quarter as we went public and drove another quarter-end record in Total Platform Assets due in large part to a second consecutive record quarter in net cross account transfers from Cash Management to Investment Advisory. We continued to expand our product suite in our effort to optimize client financial outcomes including with the launch of early access to Wealthfront Home Lending, the initial rollout of the Wealthfront Treasury Money Market Fund, and further enhancements to our core Investment Advisory and Cash Management offerings.”

Wealthfront shares fell 13% to trade at $7.31 on Thursday.

These analysts made changes to their price targets on Wealthfront following earnings announcement.

  • Keefe, Bruyette & Woods analyst Ryan Tomasello downgraded Wealthfront from Outperform to Market Perform and lowered the price target from $13.5 to $9.5.
  • Wells Fargo analyst Robert Ryan maintained the stock with an Overweight rating and lowered the price target from $12.5 to $12.

data.title: Wall Street May Be Underestimating The Next Stock Market Rally, Goldman Sachs Says
data.description:
Strategists at Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) say market positioning is quietly creating the conditions for an extreme upside move in equities, particularly if hedge funds and large institutions start rushing back into stocks.

The idea isn't that trillions of dollars will suddenly appear in the market. Instead, Goldman's trading desk says a massive amount of institutional capital is already sitting on the sidelines, waiting for the right moment to rotate back into risk assets.

If that shift begins, the result could be a sharp move higher across major indexes, and it might happen faster than many investors expect.

Wall Street Is Watching Positioning, Not Just Headlines

A recent note from Goldman's trading desk suggests hedge funds have significantly reduced exposure to equities after weeks of volatile trading. However, if markets stabilize or begin climbing again, those same funds may be forced to quickly rebuild equity exposure.

When hedge funds rush to rebuild positions at the same time, buying pressure can cascade through the market. Traders sometimes refer to this as a positioning unwind, where defensive trades are rapidly reversed. That kind of environment has historically produced violent rallies that catch the market off guard.

Recent Market Turbulence Has Kept Investors Nervous

Investors have spent the past several months wrestling with inflation concerns, shifting expectations around interest rates, and geopolitical tensions.

Those worries triggered sharp moves in U.S. equities earlier this year. At one point, the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) dropped more than 800 points in a single trading session.

The selloff was driven by losses in major financial companies, including Goldman Sachs and American Express (NYSE:AXP), highlighting how fragile investor sentiment had become during the downturn.

Episodes like that often push hedge funds and institutional investors into defensive mode. But ironically, periods of heavy selling can sometimes set the stage for powerful rebounds.

A Rally Could Spread Beyond Big Tech

For much of the past two years, the stock market's gains were heavily concentrated in a handful of technology companies linked to artificial intelligence.

In recent times, a growing number of investors are exploring companies with strong physical infrastructure and stable demand. These businesses operate in areas such as energy production, industrial manufacturing, and logistics. They are commonly referred to as HALO (Heavy Assets, Low Obsolescence) COMPANIES.

This safe-haven strategy will protect investors’ funds against overexposure to AI and Tech stocks. If institutional money starts rotating into these sectors, the next rally could involve a much larger portion of the stock market. What makes this potential rotation important is how institutional money typically moves through markets.

Large asset managers rarely shift capital into a single company. Instead, they allocate funds across entire sectors or baskets of stocks. So, if investors start trimming exposure to AI heavyweights, that capital could begin flowing into other parts of the market like energy, financials, and industrials. 

When money starts spreading across multiple sectors instead of concentrating in just a handful of tech stocks, the rally becomes broader. That kind of participation tends to lift a larger share of the stock market, not just a few headline names.

What Could Trigger The Next Big Move In Stocks

For now, the market is still waiting for a clear catalyst. Several developments could spark the kind of buying Goldman's strategists are watching for.

Cooling inflation data could convince investors that interest rates are nearing a peak. Strong corporate earnings could also reassure markets that economic growth remains intact. Even small shifts in sentiment can matter when investors are heavily positioned on the defensive.

Once the market begins climbing, momentum strategies and algorithmic trading systems can amplify the move by automatically increasing exposure to equities. That's one reason positioning-driven rallies can sometimes appear sudden and dramatic.

Is This A Clear Prediction?

Goldman Sachs isn't predicting a guaranteed surge in stocks. But the firm's trading desk believes the market may be more fragile and more explosive than it appears.

For investors watching the next move in stocks like the S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq (NDAQ), the key question may not be whether the economy improves dramatically.

It may simply be when sidelined capital decides to move back into stocks. When trillions of dollars start chasing equities at the same time, markets have a way of moving very quickly.

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: $100 Invested In Northrop Grumman 5 Years Ago Would Be Worth This Much Today
data.description:

data.title: Campbell's Analysts Slash Their Forecasts After Downbeat Q2 Results
data.description:
Campbell's Co (NASDAQ:CPB) reported worse-than-expected second-quarter financial results and lowered its FY26 adjusted EPS guidance below estimates.

Campbell's reported quarterly earnings of 51 cents per share which missed the analyst consensus estimate of 57 cents per share. The company reported quarterly sales of $2.564 billion which missed the analyst consensus estimate of $2.610 billion.

"Our core Meals & Beverages portfolio delivered in-market consumption growth in the second quarter, highlighted by the Rao's brand surpassing $1 billion in trailing twelve-month net sales. Overall results, however, fell short of our expectations due to weaker-than-expected performance in Snacks and storm-related shipment disruptions," said Mick Beekhuizen, Campbell's Chief Executive Officer. "To stabilize Snacks, we are taking decisive action, focused on sharpening our value, new product innovation, and in-market execution."

Looking ahead, Campbell's lowered its fiscal 2026 adjusted earnings guidance to a range of $2.15 to $2.25 per share. The company had previously forecast $2.40 to $2.55 per share. The revised outlook also fell below the analyst consensus estimate of $2.42.

Campbell's shares fell 1.8% to trade at $22.53 on Thursday.

These analysts made changes to their price targets on Campbell's following earnings announcement.

data.title: Fresh Economic Instability Creates Fresh Challenges For S&P 500
data.description:
The S&P 500 index continues to slide as volatility in the Middle East spreads fear among investors. 

Over the past two weeks, as the Trump administration opted for a military intervention in Iran, sparking widespread disruption throughout the Middle East, the S&P 500 has shed more than 2.5% in value while oil prices continue to surge. 

According to one report conducted by fiscal analyst Kent Smetters, the war in Iran may ultimately cost the American economy up to $210 billion. 

The United States isn't alone in becoming adversely economically affected by the war, with global energy markets and gasoline prices sparking weakness across the S&P 500's global peers. 

To make matters more complicated, the US energy department has warned that petrol and diesel prices are unlikely to fall back to prewar levels until mid-2027 at the earliest, prompting more fears over the prospect of inflation creeping back into the economy. 

Official figures now show that US petrol prices have climbed 19% over the past two weeks to $3.50 a gallon, while energy supplies have been throttled due to disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz. Likewise, diesel prices have increased 28% to $4.86 a gallon. 

Forecasts from the Energy Information Administration suggest that petrol won't drop back below its pre-conflict level of $2.94 until the end of 2027, while it predicts that diesel won't return to $3.81 until the middle of next year. 

Given the invaluable role that diesel plays in supporting US industry, prolonged price hikes could lead to long-term economic pressures that may hinder the performance of domestic markets throughout the year ahead. 

Mounting Economic Challenges

The timing of the conflict in Iran comes as the US economy is contending with mounting contractions in the domestic labor market. 

Payrolls throughout the United States fell by 92,000 in February as unemployment rose to 4.4%, according to official figures. Worryingly, this downturn has bewildered analysts, who had largely expected hiring to remain stable. 

Instead, the economy has been left to come to terms with its biggest monthly job loss since the government shut down in October. 

The figures have added fuel to concerns about the rise in oil prices in recent days, and almost every domestic sector shed jobs, with losses even experienced in the typically resilient healthcare sector. 

Federal government employment also shrank by 10,000 in February, with total jobs dropping by 330,000, or 11%, since reaching a peak in October 2024. 

Could the S&P 500 Fall Further?

Given that the S&P 500 has experienced such exponential growth in recent years following the start of the artificial intelligence boom on Wall Street, the prospect of fresh economic vulnerability in the United States has been particularly concerning for investors. 

The S&P 500's growth in recent years has led to sky-high valuations that could be more prone to economic pressures as they emerge. 

The ongoing geopolitical concerns sparked by oil price increases have a precedent in the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine, which saw a peak of $139 per barrel in the US. 

However, there are some important distinctions to make between 2026 and 2022. The Fed had the room to act decisively in hiking rates to cool inflation, which itself had been accelerated by a series of stimulus packages in the pandemic. Given that the S&P 500 surged 26% one year later in 2023 off the back of the emerging AI boom, it's clear that even deep declines can prompt fast recoveries over time. 

Looking Overseas for Resilience

Many global markets have been rocked by the escalations in the Middle East, and even China's famously resilient Hang Seng index has struggled to maintain its momentum as headwinds grow. 

March saw China set its lowest annual growth target on record, which was dropped from 5% to 4.5% off the back of tariff pressures and issues linked to deflation taking hold. 

Hong Kong's Hang Seng index fell 1.35% in the wake of the conflict in Iran, but its relative strength could still inspire more investors to look to Asia for stronger recoveries than the S&P 500. 

Thanks to a strong 2025, which saw the Hang Seng grow 30%, it's clear that China has plenty of precedent when it comes to rebounding from setbacks like tariff pressures from the United States. 

Innovations in AI have helped to support more domestic growth, while the Chinese government has implemented fresh stimulus measures in a bid to support the nation's economic recovery. 

As a result, China is benefiting from more frictionless market entry opportunities than ever before, in a boost to domestic production and supply chain resilience. 

Should the Hang Seng post a stronger recovery than the S&P 500 as the fallout from the Middle East continues to be determined, we could see more investors look to Asia as an alternative to the high valuations in the US. 

What's Next for the S&P 500?

The S&P 500's growth is becoming increasingly tied to the overall economic health of the United States, and at a time when the fallout of the conflict in Iran and unexpected job market movements are sparking fresh fears over the state of the economy in 2026, the coming weeks may be pivotal in learning the direction that the index will take over the year ahead. 

De-escalation in the Middle East can assist the Federal Reserve in maintaining a relatively loose monetary policy, while any signs of a reversal in unemployment rates will help to restore some investor confidence in US markets. 

However, with petrol and diesel prices set to remain higher for longer, we may see more investors adopt a global outlook for their portfolios as a means of balancing risk in 2026 and beyond.

Disclosure: On the date of publication, Dmytro Spilka did not hold (either directly or indirectly) any positions in the securities mentioned in this article. The opinions expressed in this article are those of the writer. Dmytro Spilka does not intend to make a trade in any of the securities mentioned above in the next 72 hours.
